claim: A video filmed at the microscopic level shows a nanobot as it “picks up a lazy sperm by the tail and inseminates an egg with it.”

result.claim: A video filmed at the microscopic level shows a nanobot as it “picks up a lazy sperm by the tail and inseminates an egg with it.”
result.reasoning: While research (Paper ID: 24) describes the development of "artificially motorized sperm cells" or "hybrid micromotors" that can "capture, transport, and release single immotile live sperm cells" (supporting the "picks up a lazy sperm by the tail" aspect of the claim), the same paper explicitly states, "Despite the fact that there still remain some challenges on the way to achieve successful fertilization with artificially motorized sperms." This directly refutes the claim that a video shows a nanobot successfully "inseminates an egg with it," as the technology itself has not yet overcome the challenges to achieve successful fertilization. Therefore, a video depicting this complete and successful process as described in the claim would be inconsistent with the current state of the research.

evidence.text: Fetal double-positive T cells (CD4+CD8+) were recovered in the maternal thymus during pregnancy.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers
